1	---
2	name: wiki
3	description: The wiki CLI and the node's two knowledge bases -- project wiki and memory.
4	---
5	
6	# Wiki
7	
8	A wiki is an indexed folder tree of `_index.md` files. A node works with two:
9	
10	- **Project wiki** (`$WIKI_DIR`) -- shared across nodes; per-branch, reaching
11	  others only through merges (use `fractal radio` for live coordination). Holds
12	  architecture, conventions, and patterns; contribute durable project-wide
13	  knowledge here.
14	- **Memory** (`$MEMORY_DIR`) -- this node's private knowledge base. See the
15	  `memory` skill for how to maintain it.
16	
17	Todo lists follow the same split: a private working checklist lives in memory; a
18	task list other nodes should see and track lives in the project wiki. Either way
19	it is living state -- current open items, pruned as they complete -- never an
20	append-only log.
21	
22	Run `wiki --help` and `wiki <command> --help` for the CLI (init, update, lint,
23	map, search, read). Always pass `--path` (`$WIKI_DIR` or `$MEMORY_DIR`) -- a
24	bare command resolves the enclosing wiki by walking up, else `{cwd}/wiki`, which
25	from the node's cwd is the project wiki at best and never memory, so an omitted
26	`--path` silently targets the wrong wiki or errors. Run
27	`wiki update --path=<dir>` after adding, moving, or deleting pages;
28	`wiki lint --path=<dir>` validates structure.
29	
30	## Editing discipline
31	
32	Name pages and folders in ascii snake_case (`command_core`, not `command-core`)
33	-- both wikis enforce identifier-safe names (the project wiki's mirror the
34	source modules they document), so `wiki lint` rejects hyphens and spaces.
35	
36	`wiki update` regenerates derived state: each page's entry line in `_index.md`
37	(name and description) is pulled from the page's own frontmatter, so fix a
38	`desc:` on the page and rerun update -- hand edits to an index line are
39	overwritten by the next update, and each page's H1 is rewritten to its name
40	unless an authored frontmatter `title:` supplies the heading, which update keeps
41	and renders as the H1 (index entry lines still show the name). `wiki lint` is
42	regenerate-and-compare: it prints the diff `wiki update` would apply plus any
43	real defects, and separates issues (must fix) from advisory notes. Work the loop
44	-- edit pages, `wiki update`, `wiki lint` -- until clean (clean = lint exits 0;
45	scripts branch on the exit code, not the prose summary); lint validates
46	structure, not content truth, so verify facts against your sources yourself.
47	
48	## Cross-linking
49	
50	Cross-reference aggressively -- the links between pages are the wiki's primary
51	value -- but **link only to pages that already exist.** Sibling nodes build
52	their pages in parallel, so a page you would link to may not exist yet: defer
53	that forward link rather than emit a wikilink to a not-yet-created page. Stale
54	sibling links are an expected transient, not a failure -- `wiki lint` reports a
55	stale link in index or page prose as an advisory note (exit 0), so never stall
56	an iteration chasing them. Broken links in the generated index link block -- the
57	rows `wiki update` maintains -- are the exception: each is a hard issue, fixed
58	by repairing the target or removing the row with `wiki update --prune`. The
59	**parent** reconciles stale sibling links when children merge up: indexes
60	refresh mechanically at commit and merge, so its integration job is repairing or
61	pruning what lint reports, not rerunning `wiki update` -- plus refreshing any
62	navigation or status tables it authored (lint cannot see a stale "in flight" row
63	for a page that has since merged).
64	
65	Wikilinks also stay inside the wiki you are writing in. A `[[...]]` link targets
66	another page in the same wiki; anything outside it -- source files, configs, or
67	the other knowledge base (project wiki vs. memory) -- is referenced in plain
68	text or backticks, never linked. `wiki lint` notes out-of-wiki wikilinks as
69	stale.
70	
71	## Structure
72	
73	Lay the wiki out as topical folders from the start: each domain area gets a
74	folder with its own `_index.md`, and pages join the folder that owns their
75	subsystem. A flat root is fine below ~6-8 pages; planning more, create the
76	folders before the pages -- the directory tree is what makes a wiki browsable,
77	searchable, and navigable, and it should mirror the domain the way a good module
78	layout mirrors a design. A shape to aim for:
79	
80	```text
81	wiki/
82	  _index.md
83	  <subsystem>/
84	    _index.md
85	    <topic>.md
86	    ...
87	  <subsystem>/
88	    _index.md
89	    <sub-area>/
90	      _index.md
91	      <topic>.md
92	      ...
93	    <sub-area>/
94	      ...
95	    <topic>.md
96	    ...
97	  ...
98	```
99	
100	Nest deeper as areas grow: when a folder accumulates pages spanning more than
101	one concern, split them into sub-folders, each with its own `_index.md`. Every
102	index is the table of contents for its level, so structured nesting is what
103	keeps the wiki navigable at any size -- a reader answers "where does this live?"
104	by descending a few links instead of scanning one long list, and related pages
105	sit beside each other where cross-links suggest themselves.
106	
107	A grown flat wiki shards into folders the same way after the fact: move related
108	pages into a subfolder and rerun `wiki update` -- indexes regenerate around the
109	new layout, and inbound links the move broke surface as stale-link notes to
110	repair. Before dumping a big tree, preflight with `wiki map --stat` (a one-line
111	size summary of what the same flags would print) and bound the dump with
112	`--depth` and `--desc-limit=<n>` -- descriptions print untruncated by default.
113

---
name: wiki
description: The wiki CLI and the node's two knowledge bases -- project wiki and memory.
---

# Wiki

A wiki is an indexed folder tree of `_index.md` files. A node works with two:

- **Project wiki** (`$WIKI_DIR`) -- shared across nodes; per-branch, reaching
  others only through merges (use `fractal radio` for live coordination). Holds
  architecture, conventions, and patterns; contribute durable project-wide
  knowledge here.
- **Memory** (`$MEMORY_DIR`) -- this node's private knowledge base. See the
  `memory` skill for how to maintain it.

Todo lists follow the same split: a private working checklist lives in memory; a
task list other nodes should see and track lives in the project wiki. Either way
it is living state -- current open items, pruned as they complete -- never an
append-only log.

Run `wiki --help` and `wiki <command> --help` for the CLI (init, update, lint,
map, search, read). Always pass `--path` (`$WIKI_DIR` or `$MEMORY_DIR`) -- a
bare command resolves the enclosing wiki by walking up, else `{cwd}/wiki`, which
from the node's cwd is the project wiki at best and never memory, so an omitted
`--path` silently targets the wrong wiki or errors. Run
`wiki update --path=<dir>` after adding, moving, or deleting pages;
`wiki lint --path=<dir>` validates structure.

## Editing discipline

Name pages and folders in ascii snake_case (`command_core`, not `command-core`)
-- both wikis enforce identifier-safe names (the project wiki's mirror the
source modules they document), so `wiki lint` rejects hyphens and spaces.

`wiki update` regenerates derived state: each page's entry line in `_index.md`
(name and description) is pulled from the page's own frontmatter, so fix a
`desc:` on the page and rerun update -- hand edits to an index line are
overwritten by the next update, and each page's H1 is rewritten to its name
unless an authored frontmatter `title:` supplies the heading, which update keeps
and renders as the H1 (index entry lines still show the name). `wiki lint` is
regenerate-and-compare: it prints the diff `wiki update` would apply plus any
real defects, and separates issues (must fix) from advisory notes. Work the loop
-- edit pages, `wiki update`, `wiki lint` -- until clean (clean = lint exits 0;
scripts branch on the exit code, not the prose summary); lint validates
structure, not content truth, so verify facts against your sources yourself.

## Cross-linking

Cross-reference aggressively -- the links between pages are the wiki's primary
value -- but **link only to pages that already exist.** Sibling nodes build
their pages in parallel, so a page you would link to may not exist yet: defer
that forward link rather than emit a wikilink to a not-yet-created page. Stale
sibling links are an expected transient, not a failure -- `wiki lint` reports a
stale link in index or page prose as an advisory note (exit 0), so never stall
an iteration chasing them. Broken links in the generated index link block -- the
rows `wiki update` maintains -- are the exception: each is a hard issue, fixed
by repairing the target or removing the row with `wiki update --prune`. The
**parent** reconciles stale sibling links when children merge up: indexes
refresh mechanically at commit and merge, so its integration job is repairing or
pruning what lint reports, not rerunning `wiki update` -- plus refreshing any
navigation or status tables it authored (lint cannot see a stale "in flight" row
for a page that has since merged).

Wikilinks also stay inside the wiki you are writing in. A `[[...]]` link targets
another page in the same wiki; anything outside it -- source files, configs, or
the other knowledge base (project wiki vs. memory) -- is referenced in plain
text or backticks, never linked. `wiki lint` notes out-of-wiki wikilinks as
stale.

## Structure

Lay the wiki out as topical folders from the start: each domain area gets a
folder with its own `_index.md`, and pages join the folder that owns their
subsystem. A flat root is fine below ~6-8 pages; planning more, create the
folders before the pages -- the directory tree is what makes a wiki browsable,
searchable, and navigable, and it should mirror the domain the way a good module
layout mirrors a design. A shape to aim for:

```text
wiki/
  _index.md
  <subsystem>/
    _index.md
    <topic>.md
    ...
  <subsystem>/
    _index.md
    <sub-area>/
      _index.md
      <topic>.md
      ...
    <sub-area>/
      ...
    <topic>.md
    ...
  ...
```

Nest deeper as areas grow: when a folder accumulates pages spanning more than
one concern, split them into sub-folders, each with its own `_index.md`. Every
index is the table of contents for its level, so structured nesting is what
keeps the wiki navigable at any size -- a reader answers "where does this live?"
by descending a few links instead of scanning one long list, and related pages
sit beside each other where cross-links suggest themselves.

A grown flat wiki shards into folders the same way after the fact: move related
pages into a subfolder and rerun `wiki update` -- indexes regenerate around the
new layout, and inbound links the move broke surface as stale-link notes to
repair. Before dumping a big tree, preflight with `wiki map --stat` (a one-line
size summary of what the same flags would print) and bound the dump with
`--depth` and `--desc-limit=<n>` -- descriptions print untruncated by default.

1	# Relay integration — research aggregate
2	
3	Synthesis of the relay-integration research requested by the root (radio
4	5CD20B25) alongside the Nostr-first output-layering decision (DESIGN.md §1,
5	decision log). Two child studies feed this aggregate:
6	
7	- **[event-kinds.md](event-kinds.md)** — the event-kind taxonomy: what
8	  Lindenmayer telemetry maps to standard NIPs vs needs custom kinds, and how
9	  custom kinds are documented for portability.
10	- **[degradation.md](degradation.md)** — the degradation analysis: what
11	  survives on a plain NIP-29 relay vs requires Buzz, and the
12	  identity/attestation story without NIP-OA enforcement.
13	
14	This summary is the interface for the future `core` node's contract; the
15	detail lives in the two studies.
16	
17	## Findings in brief
18	
19	**1. The taxonomy is small and mostly standard.** Radio traffic rides
20	standard NIPs unchanged — messages are NIP-29 kind-9 group chat (with
21	Lindenmayer's `priority`/`subject` as ignorable extra tags), reactions are
22	NIP-25, saved-sets are NIP-51 addressable lists. Eight custom kinds cover
23	everything else, split into two blocks by semantics: `420xx` append-only
24	history (node lifecycle 42010, run accounting 42020, subgraph digest 42030,
25	approval request/verdict 42040/42041, template version 42050) and `381xx`
26	addressable pointers (node state 38110, template pointer 38150). Owner
27	attestation is deliberately *not* a Lindenmayer kind — it uses Buzz's NIP-OA
28	schema as-is, which DESIGN.md §1 already names core-usable
29	(schema-on-the-wire, not relay enforcement). All numbers are proposals
30	pending collision checks, not registrations.
31	
32	**2. Privacy is designed into the taxonomy, not bolted on.** Run/step
33	accounting publishes one rolled-up event per run — never per step — and
34	ephemeral workers never author events at all; they surface only in their
35	persistent parent's subgraph digest. Step-level detail stays in Fractal's
36	SQLite. This makes "aggregates flow up, details stay in the subgraph"
37	(§6.1) a property of the wire format itself.
38	
39	**3. Everything Lindenmayer *records* survives a plain relay; what degrades
40	is *enforcement*.** On a strfry/khatru-class relay speaking NIP-01/29/42,
41	all core flows store, version, and stay queryable/attestable. What's lost is
42	relay-side enforcement: approval-gated merges become records without gates,
43	identity revocation becomes a fact readers must check rather than an access
44	cutoff, and the packaged human surfaces (desktop app, directory, huddles)
45	disappear — though any NIP-29 client can still perform the same functions.
46	
47	**4. The one degradation that is not cosmetic: private-group read gating.**
48	NIP-29's `private` flag ("only members can read") is spec-permissive —
49	relays *should* enforce it, and mainstream implementations do, but it is not
50	a protocol guarantee the way write-side moderation checks are. If a relay
51	stores the flag without enforcing it, the aggregates-up privacy default
52	silently degrades from a structural boundary to a client-side courtesy.
53	NIP-42 does not close the gap: it authenticates readers but leaves
54	authorization entirely to relay policy.
55	
56	**5. Attestation without NIP-OA enforcement has a workable minimum.** The
57	bridge refuses to publish for revoked keys (closes new activity at the
58	source), and readers/extraction pipelines filter by attestation validity at
59	query time (bounds trust in stored history). This reproduces Buzz's outcome
60	with higher latency and procedural rather than structural guarantees.
61	Training-corpus safety (§4) is preserved provided the extraction pipeline
62	applies the attestation-validity filter itself instead of assuming the relay
63	did — and labels corpus rows with attestation state at extraction time.
64	
65	## Recommendations for the core node's contract
66	
67	- **Minimum relay contract:** assume NIP-01 + NIP-29 + NIP-42 and nothing
68	  more. NIP-34, Blossom, and Buzz custom-kind relay behavior are additive,
69	  never baseline.
70	- **Verify, don't infer, private-group enforcement:** treat relay-enforced
71	  `private` read gating as a per-deployment capability — a startup/config
72	  check or explicit operator attestation — and fail loud (refuse to mark a
73	  channel private) when it can't be confirmed. Buzz satisfies this by
74	  construction; "NIP-29 compliant" alone does not.
75	- **Ship with the acceptable degradations documented:** merge-gate
76	  enforcement is a bridge/process concern layered above the signed approval
77	  events; revocation latency under bridge-refusal + reader-filtering is
78	  documented, not hidden; human surfaces are pure UX with any NIP-29 client
79	  as substitute.
80	- **Custom kinds are self-documenting:** each carries the documentation
81	  convention from event-kinds.md §4 so any Nostr client can interpret them
82	  without Lindenmayer context; kind numbers get a collision check against
83	  Buzz's allocations before first publish (open in DESIGN.md §8).
84

# Relay integration — research aggregate

Synthesis of the relay-integration research requested by the root (radio
5CD20B25) alongside the Nostr-first output-layering decision (DESIGN.md §1,
decision log). Two child studies feed this aggregate:

- **[event-kinds.md](event-kinds.md)** — the event-kind taxonomy: what
  Lindenmayer telemetry maps to standard NIPs vs needs custom kinds, and how
  custom kinds are documented for portability.
- **[degradation.md](degradation.md)** — the degradation analysis: what
  survives on a plain NIP-29 relay vs requires Buzz, and the
  identity/attestation story without NIP-OA enforcement.

This summary is the interface for the future `core` node's contract; the
detail lives in the two studies.

## Findings in brief

**1. The taxonomy is small and mostly standard.** Radio traffic rides
standard NIPs unchanged — messages are NIP-29 kind-9 group chat (with
Lindenmayer's `priority`/`subject` as ignorable extra tags), reactions are
NIP-25, saved-sets are NIP-51 addressable lists. Eight custom kinds cover
everything else, split into two blocks by semantics: `420xx` append-only
history (node lifecycle 42010, run accounting 42020, subgraph digest 42030,
approval request/verdict 42040/42041, template version 42050) and `381xx`
addressable pointers (node state 38110, template pointer 38150). Owner
attestation is deliberately *not* a Lindenmayer kind — it uses Buzz's NIP-OA
schema as-is, which DESIGN.md §1 already names core-usable
(schema-on-the-wire, not relay enforcement). All numbers are proposals
pending collision checks, not registrations.

**2. Privacy is designed into the taxonomy, not bolted on.** Run/step
accounting publishes one rolled-up event per run — never per step — and
ephemeral workers never author events at all; they surface only in their
persistent parent's subgraph digest. Step-level detail stays in Fractal's
SQLite. This makes "aggregates flow up, details stay in the subgraph"
(§6.1) a property of the wire format itself.

**3. Everything Lindenmayer *records* survives a plain relay; what degrades
is *enforcement*.** On a strfry/khatru-class relay speaking NIP-01/29/42,
all core flows store, version, and stay queryable/attestable. What's lost is
relay-side enforcement: approval-gated merges become records without gates,
identity revocation becomes a fact readers must check rather than an access
cutoff, and the packaged human surfaces (desktop app, directory, huddles)
disappear — though any NIP-29 client can still perform the same functions.

**4. The one degradation that is not cosmetic: private-group read gating.**
NIP-29's `private` flag ("only members can read") is spec-permissive —
relays *should* enforce it, and mainstream implementations do, but it is not
a protocol guarantee the way write-side moderation checks are. If a relay
stores the flag without enforcing it, the aggregates-up privacy default
silently degrades from a structural boundary to a client-side courtesy.
NIP-42 does not close the gap: it authenticates readers but leaves
authorization entirely to relay policy.

**5. Attestation without NIP-OA enforcement has a workable minimum.** The
bridge refuses to publish for revoked keys (closes new activity at the
source), and readers/extraction pipelines filter by attestation validity at
query time (bounds trust in stored history). This reproduces Buzz's outcome
with higher latency and procedural rather than structural guarantees.
Training-corpus safety (§4) is preserved provided the extraction pipeline
applies the attestation-validity filter itself instead of assuming the relay
did — and labels corpus rows with attestation state at extraction time.

## Recommendations for the core node's contract

- **Minimum relay contract:** assume NIP-01 + NIP-29 + NIP-42 and nothing
  more. NIP-34, Blossom, and Buzz custom-kind relay behavior are additive,
  never baseline.
- **Verify, don't infer, private-group enforcement:** treat relay-enforced
  `private` read gating as a per-deployment capability — a startup/config
  check or explicit operator attestation — and fail loud (refuse to mark a
  channel private) when it can't be confirmed. Buzz satisfies this by
  construction; "NIP-29 compliant" alone does not.
- **Ship with the acceptable degradations documented:** merge-gate
  enforcement is a bridge/process concern layered above the signed approval
  events; revocation latency under bridge-refusal + reader-filtering is
  documented, not hidden; human surfaces are pure UX with any NIP-29 client
  as substitute.
- **Custom kinds are self-documenting:** each carries the documentation
  convention from event-kinds.md §4 so any Nostr client can interpret them
  without Lindenmayer context; kind numbers get a collision check against
  Buzz's allocations before first publish (open in DESIGN.md §8).

content.content:
154	## 6. Design principles
155	
156	1. **Privacy-preserving defaults.** Human employees' full potential on this
157	   platform is only unlocked with a privacy-first approach; the goal is a
158	   collaboration platform for humans and AI as one integrated organizational
159	   graph. No arbitrary search of histories: access follows the graph —
160	   aggregates flow up, details stay in the subgraph. Privacy is a default,
161	   not a feature. Whether a subgraph publishes its raw node-session
162	   transcripts is a per-subgraph platform parameter (same family as the
163	   governance-mode parameter, §6.4); the platform-wide default is
164	   private-in-subgraph.
165	2. **No new storage systems.** The signed event log is the history. Retention
166	   is relay policy, per channel. Source-of-truth data stays in Fractal's
167	   SQLite and the Buzz relay; parquet files in `data/` are snapshot exports
168	   for local review (duckdb), never sources of truth.
169	3. **Never patch or fork Fractal.** Integrate only through its extension
170	   surfaces; add no capabilities to Fractal nodes.
171	4. **Human governance is explicit and traceable.** Design review is a veto by
172	   default (a platform parameter); disagreements resolve as recorded
173	   conversation, never silent override.
174	5. **Relay enforcement is an optimization, never an assumption.** Client-side
175	   verification from the signed log is the security boundary. Buzz's
176	   relay-side enforcement — instant revocation cutoff, approval-gated
177	   merges, membership-checked writes, private-group read gating — is
178	   defense-in-depth that makes enforcement cheap when Buzz is deployed
179	   alongside, but it is Buzz-layer under §1's layering rule, and no client,
180	   bridge, or consumer may reason from it. Every consumer verifies from the
181	   signed log alone: attestation chains are checked before an author is
182	   trusted, approvals are counted before a merge is treated as approved,
183	   revoked keys are filtered at read time, and private-group read gating is
184	   treated as unverified until the deployment proves it. (Detail and the
185	   minimum relay contract: the relay-integration research aggregate,
186	   `docs/research/relay-integration/README.md`.)
187	
188	### Shadow cost
189	
190	All dollar figures in this project are *shadow costs*: real, server-reported
191	token counts priced at public API rates (Fractal's LiteLLM pricing table),
192	while execution actually runs on a Claude Max subscription. Nothing was
193	billed at these prices.
194	
195	- Token counts are ground truth (reported by the API in stream events, never
196	  estimated locally). Dollar figures are derived, notional, API-equivalent
197	  prices.
198	- Fractal's budget primitives (`max_cost`, reserve mode, cascading finish)
199	  operate on shadow cost and remain fully functional as steering and safety
200	  mechanisms — a budget cap is a real cap on work, even if the dollars are
201	  notional.
202	- Shadow cost is the right unit for the control plane's analytics: it is
203	  comparable across nodes, templates, models, and time, independent of how
204	  any given org pays (subscription, API, or enterprise agreement).
205	- The binding constraint on subscription execution is not dollars but rate
206	  windows; throttling events are recorded and reported separately from cost.
207	- Every exported dataset (parquet snapshots, demo dashboards) labels cost
208	  columns as shadow cost. No figure is presented as actual spend.
209	
210	## 7. Development & demo strategy
211	
212	- Developed by a Fractal tree from day one; the demo observes the tree that
213	  builds Lindenmayer, so dashboards show real subgraphs, merge-acceptance,

## 6. Design principles

1. **Privacy-preserving defaults.** Human employees' full potential on this
   platform is only unlocked with a privacy-first approach; the goal is a
   collaboration platform for humans and AI as one integrated organizational
   graph. No arbitrary search of histories: access follows the graph —
   aggregates flow up, details stay in the subgraph. Privacy is a default,
   not a feature. Whether a subgraph publishes its raw node-session
   transcripts is a per-subgraph platform parameter (same family as the
   governance-mode parameter, §6.4); the platform-wide default is
   private-in-subgraph.
2. **No new storage systems.** The signed event log is the history. Retention
   is relay policy, per channel. Source-of-truth data stays in Fractal's
   SQLite and the Buzz relay; parquet files in `data/` are snapshot exports
   for local review (duckdb), never sources of truth.
3. **Never patch or fork Fractal.** Integrate only through its extension
   surfaces; add no capabilities to Fractal nodes.
4. **Human governance is explicit and traceable.** Design review is a veto by
   default (a platform parameter); disagreements resolve as recorded
   conversation, never silent override.
5. **Relay enforcement is an optimization, never an assumption.** Client-side
   verification from the signed log is the security boundary. Buzz's
   relay-side enforcement — instant revocation cutoff, approval-gated
   merges, membership-checked writes, private-group read gating — is
   defense-in-depth that makes enforcement cheap when Buzz is deployed
   alongside, but it is Buzz-layer under §1's layering rule, and no client,
   bridge, or consumer may reason from it. Every consumer verifies from the
   signed log alone: attestation chains are checked before an author is
   trusted, approvals are counted before a merge is treated as approved,
   revoked keys are filtered at read time, and private-group read gating is
   treated as unverified until the deployment proves it. (Detail and the
   minimum relay contract: the relay-integration research aggregate,
   `docs/research/relay-integration/README.md`.)

### Shadow cost

All dollar figures in this project are *shadow costs*: real, server-reported
token counts priced at public API rates (Fractal's LiteLLM pricing table),
while execution actually runs on a Claude Max subscription. Nothing was
billed at these prices.

- Token counts are ground truth (reported by the API in stream events, never
  estimated locally). Dollar figures are derived, notional, API-equivalent
  prices.
- Fractal's budget primitives (`max_cost`, reserve mode, cascading finish)
  operate on shadow cost and remain fully functional as steering and safety
  mechanisms — a budget cap is a real cap on work, even if the dollars are
  notional.
- Shadow cost is the right unit for the control plane's analytics: it is
  comparable across nodes, templates, models, and time, independent of how
  any given org pays (subscription, API, or enterprise agreement).
- The binding constraint on subscription execution is not dollars but rate
  windows; throttling events are recorded and reported separately from cost.
- Every exported dataset (parquet snapshots, demo dashboards) labels cost
  columns as shadow cost. No figure is presented as actual spend.

## 7. Development & demo strategy

- Developed by a Fractal tree from day one; the demo observes the tree that
  builds Lindenmayer, so dashboards show real subgraphs, merge-acceptance,

1	"""NIP-01 event model — the trust root of the platform.
2	
3	Canonical id computation, signing, and verification for Nostr events.
4	Every other Lindenmayer component consumes events through this module;
5	nothing here knows about Lindenmayer kinds, relays, or Buzz.
6	
7	Canonical serialization follows NIP-01: the id is the SHA-256 of the
8	UTF-8 JSON array ``[0, pubkey, created_at, kind, tags, content]`` with no
9	whitespace. Python's ``json.dumps(ensure_ascii=False, separators=(",", ":"))``
10	produces the same escaping as ``JSON.stringify`` (the de-facto reference
11	via nostr-tools), which is what published event ids are computed with.
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	import hashlib
17	import json
18	import time
19	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
20	from typing import Any, Iterator
21	
22	import lindenmayer.core.keys as _keys
23	
24	__all__ = ["Event", "EventValidationError", "canonical_serialization", "compute_event_id"]
25	
26	
27	class EventValidationError(ValueError):
28	    """Raised when an event fails structural validation."""
29	
30	
31	def canonical_serialization(
32	    pubkey: str, created_at: int, kind: int, tags: list[list[str]], content: str
33	) -> bytes:
34	    """The NIP-01 canonical byte serialization whose SHA-256 is the event id."""
35	    return json.dumps(
36	        [0, pubkey, created_at, kind, tags, content],
37	        ensure_ascii=False,
38	        separators=(",", ":"),
39	    ).encode("utf-8")
40	
41	
42	def compute_event_id(
43	    pubkey: str, created_at: int, kind: int, tags: list[list[str]], content: str
44	) -> str:
45	    """Lowercase hex SHA-256 of the canonical serialization."""
46	    return hashlib.sha256(
47	        canonical_serialization(pubkey, created_at, kind, tags, content)
48	    ).hexdigest()
49	
50	
51	def _is_hex(value: str, length: int) -> bool:
52	    if len(value) != length or value != value.lower():
53	        return False
54	    try:
55	        bytes.fromhex(value)
56	    except ValueError:
57	        return False
58	    return True
59	
60	
61	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
62	class Event:
63	    """An immutable NIP-01 event.
64	
65	    ``tags`` is stored as a tuple of tuples so instances are hashable; the
66	    wire form (``to_dict``/``to_json``) uses lists per NIP-01.
67	    """
68	
69	    pubkey: str
70	    created_at: int
71	    kind: int
72	    tags: tuple[tuple[str, ...], ...]
73	    content: str
74	    id: str
75	    sig: str | None = field(default=None)
76	
77	    # -- construction ------------------------------------------------------
78	
79	    @classmethod
80	    def build(
81	        cls,
82	        *,
83	        pubkey: str,
84	        kind: int,
85	        tags: list[list[str]] | tuple[tuple[str, ...], ...] = (),
86	        content: str = "",
87	        created_at: int | None = None,
88	    ) -> "Event":
89	        """Build an unsigned event with a freshly computed id."""
90	        ts = int(time.time()) if created_at is None else int(created_at)
91	        tag_lists = [list(t) for t in tags]
92	        event_id = compute_event_id(pubkey, ts, kind, tag_lists, content)
93	        return cls(
94	            pubkey=pubkey,
95	            created_at=ts,
96	            kind=kind,
97	            tags=tuple(tuple(t) for t in tags),
98	            content=content,
99	            id=event_id,
100	        )
101	
102	    @classmethod
103	    def from_dict(cls, data: dict[str, Any]) -> "Event":
104	        """Parse a wire-format event dict, validating structure (not signature)."""
105	        try:
106	            pubkey = data["pubkey"]
107	            created_at = data["created_at"]
108	            kind = data["kind"]
109	            tags = data["tags"]
110	            content = data["content"]
111	            event_id = data["id"]
112	        except (KeyError, TypeError) as exc:
113	            raise EventValidationError(f"missing event field: {exc}") from exc
114	        sig = data.get("sig")
115	        if not isinstance(pubkey, str) or not _is_hex(pubkey, 64):
116	            raise EventValidationError("pubkey must be 64-char lowercase hex")
117	        if not isinstance(event_id, str) or not _is_hex(event_id, 64):
118	            raise EventValidationError("id must be 64-char lowercase hex")
119	        if sig is not None and (not isinstance(sig, str) or not _is_hex(sig, 128)):
120	            raise EventValidationError("sig must be 128-char lowercase hex")
121	        if not isinstance(created_at, int) or isinstance(created_at, bool):
122	            raise EventValidationError("created_at must be an integer")
123	        if not isinstance(kind, int) or isinstance(kind, bool) or not 0 <= kind <= 65535:
124	            raise EventValidationError("kind must be an integer in [0, 65535]")
125	        if not isinstance(content, str):
126	            raise EventValidationError("content must be a string")
127	        if not isinstance(tags, list) or any(
128	            not isinstance(t, list) or any(not isinstance(v, str) for v in t) for t in tags
129	        ):
130	            raise EventValidationError("tags must be a list of lists of strings")
131	        return cls(
132	            pubkey=pubkey,
133	            created_at=created_at,
134	            kind=kind,
135	            tags=tuple(tuple(t) for t in tags),
136	            content=content,
137	            id=event_id,
138	            sig=sig,
139	        )
140	
141	    @classmethod
142	    def from_json(cls, raw: str | bytes) -> "Event":
143	        try:
144	            data = json.loads(raw)
145	        except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
146	            raise EventValidationError(f"invalid JSON: {exc}") from exc
147	        if not isinstance(data, dict):
148	            raise EventValidationError("event JSON must be an object")
149	        return cls.from_dict(data)
150	
151	    # -- serialization -----------------------------------------------------
152	
153	    def to_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
154	        data: dict[str, Any] = {
155	            "id": self.id,
156	            "pubkey": self.pubkey,
157	            "created_at": self.created_at,
158	            "kind": self.kind,
159	            "tags": [list(t) for t in self.tags],
160	            "content": self.content,
161	        }
162	        if self.sig is not None:
163	            data["sig"] = self.sig
164	        return data
165	
166	    def to_json(self) -> str:
167	        return json.dumps(self.to_dict(), ensure_ascii=False, separators=(",", ":"))
168	
169	    # -- integrity ---------------------------------------------------------
170	
171	    def recompute_id(self) -> str:
172	        return compute_event_id(
173	            self.pubkey, self.created_at, self.kind, [list(t) for t in self.tags], self.content
174	        )
175	
176	    def id_valid(self) -> bool:
177	        """Does the declared id match the canonical serialization?"""
178	        return self.id == self.recompute_id()
179	
180	    def sig_valid(self) -> bool:
181	        """Is the signature a valid BIP-340 signature by ``pubkey`` over ``id``?
182	
183	        False when unsigned. Does not check the id itself — use ``verify``.
184	        """
185	        if self.sig is None:
186	            return False
187	        return _keys.schnorr_verify(self.pubkey, bytes.fromhex(self.id), bytes.fromhex(self.sig))
188	
189	    def verify(self) -> bool:
190	        """Full NIP-01 verification: id matches content AND signature is valid.
191	
192	        This is the platform's trust root — every consumer that reads from a
193	        relay must call this (or a helper that does) before believing an event.
194	        """
195	        return self.id_valid() and self.sig_valid()
196	
197	    # -- tag access --------------------------------------------------------
198	
199	    def tag_values(self, name: str) -> Iterator[tuple[str, ...]]:
200	        """Yield the value portions (everything after the name) of matching tags."""
201	        for tag in self.tags:
202	            if tag and tag[0] == name:
203	                yield tag[1:]
204	
205	    def first_tag_value(self, name: str) -> str | None:
206	        """The first value of the first tag named ``name``, or None."""
207	        for values in self.tag_values(name):
208	            return values[0] if values else None
209	        return None
210

1	"""secp256k1 key handling: BIP-340 Schnorr signing and verification.
2	
3	Thin wrapper over coincurve (libsecp256k1 — the BIP-340 reference C
4	implementation). This module is the only place in Lindenmayer that touches
5	raw key material; everything else works with hex pubkeys and `Event`s.
6	
7	Key-material discipline: secrets live in memory as bytes, are never logged,
8	and never appear in repr/str output.
9	"""
10	
11	from __future__ import annotations
12	
13	from coincurve.keys import PrivateKey, PublicKeyXOnly
14	
15	__all__ = ["Keypair", "schnorr_sign", "schnorr_verify"]
16	
17	
18	def schnorr_verify(pubkey_hex: str, message: bytes, signature: bytes) -> bool:
19	    """Verify a BIP-340 Schnorr signature by an x-only pubkey over ``message``.
20	
21	    Returns False (never raises) on malformed keys or signatures — callers
22	    treat verification failure uniformly regardless of why the input is bad.
23	    """
24	    try:
25	        pubkey = PublicKeyXOnly(bytes.fromhex(pubkey_hex))
26	        return pubkey.verify(signature, message)
27	    except Exception:
28	        return False
29	
30	
31	def schnorr_sign(secret: bytes, message: bytes, aux_rand: bytes | None = None) -> bytes:
32	    """Produce a BIP-340 Schnorr signature over ``message``.
33	
34	    ``aux_rand`` is the 32-byte auxiliary randomness from BIP-340; None lets
35	    the backend choose (test vectors pass it explicitly).
36	    """
37	    key = PrivateKey(secret)
38	    if aux_rand is None:
39	        return key.sign_schnorr(message)
40	    return key.sign_schnorr(message, aux_randomness=aux_rand)
41	
42	
43	class Keypair:
44	    """A secp256k1 keypair identified by its x-only public key (hex)."""
45	
46	    __slots__ = ("_private",)
47	
48	    def __init__(self, secret: bytes) -> None:
49	        if len(secret) != 32:
50	            raise ValueError("secret key must be exactly 32 bytes")
51	        self._private = PrivateKey(secret)
52	
53	    @classmethod
54	    def generate(cls) -> "Keypair":
55	        return cls(PrivateKey().secret)
56	
57	    @classmethod
58	    def from_hex(cls, secret_hex: str) -> "Keypair":
59	        return cls(bytes.fromhex(secret_hex))
60	
61	    @property
62	    def public_key_hex(self) -> str:
63	        """The 64-char lowercase hex x-only public key (the Nostr pubkey)."""
64	        return self._private.public_key_xonly.format().hex()
65	
66	    def sign(self, message: bytes, aux_rand: bytes | None = None) -> bytes:
67	        if aux_rand is None:
68	            return self._private.sign_schnorr(message)
69	        return self._private.sign_schnorr(message, aux_randomness=aux_rand)
70	
71	    def sign_event(self, event):  # noqa: ANN001, ANN201 — Event typed at call sites; avoids import cycle
72	        """Return a signed copy of ``event`` authored by this keypair.
73	
74	        The event's pubkey must already be this keypair's pubkey (the id
75	        commits to the pubkey, so signing someone else's event body would
76	        just produce an invalid event).
77	        """
78	        from dataclasses import replace
79	
80	        if event.pubkey != self.public_key_hex:
81	            raise ValueError("event.pubkey does not match this keypair")
82	        if not event.id_valid():
83	            raise ValueError("event id does not match its canonical serialization")
84	        signature = self.sign(bytes.fromhex(event.id))
85	        return replace(event, sig=signature.hex())
86	
87	    def __repr__(self) -> str:  # never leak the secret
88	        return f"Keypair(pubkey={self.public_key_hex})"
89

1	"""Deployment configuration: relay URL, key material, capability attestations.
2	
3	No new storage systems (DESIGN.md §6.2): configuration is a TOML file read
4	with stdlib ``tomllib`` plus environment variables. Key material is never
5	stored in the config file itself — the file names *where* the key lives
6	(an env var or a file path), and ``load_keypair`` resolves it at runtime.
7	
8	Capability attestations are the "verify, don't infer" mechanism from the
9	relay-integration research: relay-side behaviors that degrade silently on a
10	plain NIP-29 relay (private-group read gating above all) must be explicitly
11	attested by the deployment operator before the library will rely on them.
12	Absence of an attestation is a hard "no", not a "probably".
13	"""
14	
15	from __future__ import annotations
16	
17	import os
18	import tomllib
19	from pathlib import Path
20	
21	from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, field_validator
22	
23	from lindenmayer.core.keys import Keypair
24	
25	__all__ = [
26	    "CAP_PRIVATE_READ_GATING",
27	    "CapabilityAttestation",
28	    "ConfigError",
29	    "CoreConfig",
30	]
31	
32	# The one degradation that is not cosmetic (research aggregate, finding 4):
33	# relay-side enforcement of NIP-29 `private` read gating. Buzz satisfies it
34	# by construction; "NIP-29 compliant" alone does not.
35	CAP_PRIVATE_READ_GATING = "nip29-private-read-gating"
36	
37	
38	class ConfigError(ValueError):
39	    """Raised when configuration is missing, malformed, or unresolvable."""
40	
41	
42	class CapabilityAttestation(BaseModel):
43	    """An operator's explicit statement that this deployment's relay enforces
44	    a capability that the protocol alone does not guarantee.
45	
46	    This is a config-level record of an operational fact, attributable to
47	    whoever operates the deployment — not a signed wire event. Consumers
48	    that need wire-level proof verify from the signed log (verify module).
49	    """
50	
51	    capability: str
52	    attested_by: str = Field(description="operator identity: name or pubkey hex")
53	    attested_at: int = Field(description="unix timestamp of the attestation")
54	    note: str = ""
55	
56	    @field_validator("capability", "attested_by")
57	    @classmethod
58	    def _non_empty(cls, value: str) -> str:
59	        if not value.strip():
60	            raise ValueError("must be non-empty")
61	        return value
62	
63	
64	class CoreConfig(BaseModel):
65	    """Top-level configuration for a Lindenmayer deployment."""
66	
67	    relay_url: str
68	    capability_attestations: list[CapabilityAttestation] = Field(default_factory=list)
69	    secret_key_env: str = "LINDENMAYER_SECRET_KEY"
70	    secret_key_file: Path | None = None
71	
72	    @field_validator("relay_url")
73	    @classmethod
74	    def _ws_url(cls, value: str) -> str:
75	        if not value.startswith(("ws://", "wss://")):
76	            raise ValueError("relay_url must be a ws:// or wss:// URL")
77	        return value
78	
79	    # -- capabilities ------------------------------------------------------
80	
81	    def has_capability(self, capability: str) -> bool:
82	        """True only if the deployment explicitly attests ``capability``."""
83	        return any(a.capability == capability for a in self.capability_attestations)
84	
85	    def attestation_for(self, capability: str) -> CapabilityAttestation | None:
86	        for attestation in self.capability_attestations:
87	            if attestation.capability == capability:
88	                return attestation
89	        return None
90	
91	    # -- key material ------------------------------------------------------
92	
93	    def load_keypair(self) -> Keypair:
94	        """Resolve key material: env var first, then key file.
95	
96	        The secret is 64-char hex. It never transits the config file; a
97	        world-readable key file is refused outright.
98	        """
99	        secret_hex = os.environ.get(self.secret_key_env, "").strip()
100	        if secret_hex:
101	            return self._keypair_from_hex(secret_hex, source=f"${self.secret_key_env}")
102	        if self.secret_key_file is not None:
103	            path = self.secret_key_file.expanduser()
104	            if not path.is_file():
105	                raise ConfigError(f"secret key file not found: {path}")
106	            mode = path.stat().st_mode & 0o777
107	            if mode & 0o077:
108	                raise ConfigError(
109	                    f"secret key file {path} is group/world-accessible "
110	                    f"(mode {mode:o}); chmod 600 it"
111	                )
112	            return self._keypair_from_hex(path.read_text().strip(), source=str(path))
113	        raise ConfigError(
114	            f"no key material: ${self.secret_key_env} is unset and no "
115	            "secret_key_file is configured"
116	        )
117	
118	    @staticmethod
119	    def _keypair_from_hex(secret_hex: str, *, source: str) -> Keypair:
120	        try:
121	            return Keypair.from_hex(secret_hex)
122	        except ValueError as exc:
123	            # Deliberately excludes the material itself from the message.
124	            raise ConfigError(f"invalid secret key from {source}: {exc}") from exc
125	
126	    # -- loading -----------------------------------------------------------
127	
128	    @classmethod
129	    def from_toml(cls, path: str | Path) -> "CoreConfig":
130	        path = Path(path)
131	        try:
132	            data = tomllib.loads(path.read_text())
133	        except (OSError, tomllib.TOMLDecodeError) as exc:
134	            raise ConfigError(f"cannot read config {path}: {exc}") from exc
135	        try:
136	            return cls.model_validate(data)
137	        except ValueError as exc:
138	            raise ConfigError(f"invalid config {path}: {exc}") from exc
139

"""lindenmayer.core — the foundation library every other component consumes.

Module map (see README.md in this package for the full design posture):

- ``event``  — NIP-01 events: canonical id, signing, verification (trust root)
- ``keys``   — secp256k1 keypairs, BIP-340 Schnorr primitives
- ``kinds``  — typed models for Lindenmayer's custom event kinds
- ``relay``  — minimum-contract relay client (NIP-01 + NIP-29 + NIP-42)
- ``verify`` — the security boundary: attestation, approvals, revocation
- ``config`` — deployment configuration and capability attestations
"""

from lindenmayer.core.config import (
    CAP_PRIVATE_READ_GATING,
    CapabilityAttestation,
    ConfigError,
    CoreConfig,
)
from lindenmayer.core.event import (
    Event,
    EventValidationError,
    canonical_serialization,
    compute_event_id,
)
from lindenmayer.core.keys import Keypair, schnorr_sign, schnorr_verify

__all__ = [
    "CAP_PRIVATE_READ_GATING",
    "CapabilityAttestation",
    "ConfigError",
    "CoreConfig",
    "Event",
    "EventValidationError",
    "Keypair",
    "canonical_serialization",
    "compute_event_id",
    "schnorr_sign",
    "schnorr_verify",
]

content.content:
1	# lindenmayer.core
2	
3	The foundation library for Lindenmayer: NIP-01 events, Lindenmayer's custom
4	event kinds, a minimum-contract relay client, and the verification helpers
5	that let any consumer trust the signed log without trusting a relay.
6	
7	Design inputs, in authority order: `docs/DESIGN.md` (§1 layering, §4
8	extraction pipeline, §6 principles), then the relay-integration research
9	(`docs/research/relay-integration/`). The per-kind wire specs live in
10	`docs/kinds/` and are readable without any Lindenmayer context.
11	
12	## Modules
13	
14	| Module | Role |
15	|---|---|
16	| `event` | NIP-01 events: canonical id, BIP-340 signing/verification. The trust root — everything else consumes events through it. |
17	| `keys` | secp256k1 keypairs and Schnorr primitives (coincurve/libsecp256k1). The only module that touches raw key material. |
18	| `kinds` | Typed, validating models for the eight Lindenmayer kinds (42010/42020/42030/42040/42041/42050 history; 38110/38150 addressable). |
19	| `relay` | Relay client. Minimum contract: NIP-01 + NIP-29 + NIP-42 — connect, auth, publish, subscribe, query — and nothing more. |
20	| `verify` | The security boundary (DESIGN.md §6.5): attestation validation, approval counting, read-time revocation filtering, extraction-time labeling. |
21	| `config` | Relay URL, key-material resolution, per-deployment capability attestations. TOML + env only — no new storage systems (§6.2). |
22	
23	## Security posture
24	
25	**Verify from the signed log alone.** Relay-side enforcement is an
26	optimization, never an assumption. Every consumer verifies events
27	(`Event.verify()`), validates attestations, and counts approvals itself via
28	the `verify` module. A deployment on Buzz gets enforcement as a bonus; a
29	deployment on a plain NIP-29 relay loses no *integrity*, only convenience.
30	
31	**Capabilities are attested, not inferred.** Relay behaviors the protocol
32	does not guarantee — above all NIP-29 private-group *read* gating — must be
33	explicitly attested per deployment in config (`CAP_PRIVATE_READ_GATING`).
34	The relay client refuses to treat a channel as private until the deployment
35	attests enforcement: fail loud, never degrade silently.
36	
37	**Privacy is a wire-format property.** Run accounting publishes one rolled-up
38	event per run — never per step; ephemeral workers author no events and
39	surface only in their persistent parent's subgraph digest. The API is shaped
40	so the private thing is the easy thing: there is no per-step publish surface
41	to misuse.
42	
43	**Owner attestation is NIP-OA, reused as-is.** Lindenmayer mints no kind for
44	it — NIP-OA is an `auth` *tag* attachable to any event. The upstream schema
45	is restated in `docs/kinds/nip-oa-attestation.md` so draft churn cannot
46	retroactively change what historical attestations meant.
47	
48	## Acceptable degradation (plain NIP-29 relay vs Buzz)
49	
50	Per the relay-integration research (aggregate, recommendation 3), the
51	following degradations are accepted and documented rather than papered over:
52	
53	- **Merge-gate enforcement is not this library's concern.** Approval
54	  request/verdict events (42040/42041) are signed records; *enforcement* of
55	  approval-gated merges is a bridge/process concern layered above them
56	  (Buzz's `buzz-protect` where present, process discipline elsewhere). The
57	  signed chain is complete either way — what varies is whether anything
58	  mechanically blocks a merge that ignores it.
59	- **Revocation has latency under bridge-refusal + reader-filtering.** Without
60	  relay-side identity enforcement, revoking a key means: the bridge refuses
61	  to publish for revoked keys (stops new activity at the source), and readers
62	  filter by attestation validity at query time (`verify` module — bounds
63	  trust in stored history). This reproduces Buzz's outcome with higher
64	  latency and procedural rather than structural guarantees. That latency
65	  window is real; consumers that care must apply read-time filtering — the
66	  library ships it, but cannot make a relay un-serve stale events.
67	- **Human surfaces are pure UX.** The packaged surfaces (desktop app,
68	  directory, huddles) are conveniences; any NIP-29 client can perform the
69	  same functions against the same events. Nothing recorded depends on them.
70	
71	What does *not* degrade: everything Lindenmayer records — lifecycle history,
72	run accounting, digests, approval chains, template versions, attestations —
73	stores, verifies, and stays queryable on any NIP-01+29+42 relay.
74	
75	## Key handling
76	
77	Secrets resolve at runtime from an env var or a `chmod 600` key file
78	(`CoreConfig.load_keypair`); they never appear in config files, logs, or
79	reprs. Signing happens only in `keys` (libsecp256k1 via coincurve).
80

file.content:
# lindenmayer.core

The foundation library for Lindenmayer: NIP-01 events, Lindenmayer's custom
event kinds, a minimum-contract relay client, and the verification helpers
that let any consumer trust the signed log without trusting a relay.

Design inputs, in authority order: `docs/DESIGN.md` (§1 layering, §4
extraction pipeline, §6 principles), then the relay-integration research
(`docs/research/relay-integration/`). The per-kind wire specs live in
`docs/kinds/` and are readable without any Lindenmayer context.

## Modules

| Module | Role |
|---|---|
| `event` | NIP-01 events: canonical id, BIP-340 signing/verification. The trust root — everything else consumes events through it. |
| `keys` | secp256k1 keypairs and Schnorr primitives (coincurve/libsecp256k1). The only module that touches raw key material. |
| `kinds` | Typed, validating models for the eight Lindenmayer kinds (42010/42020/42030/42040/42041/42050 history; 38110/38150 addressable). |
| `relay` | Relay client. Minimum contract: NIP-01 + NIP-29 + NIP-42 — connect, auth, publish, subscribe, query — and nothing more. |
| `verify` | The security boundary (DESIGN.md §6.5): attestation validation, approval counting, read-time revocation filtering, extraction-time labeling. |
| `config` | Relay URL, key-material resolution, per-deployment capability attestations. TOML + env only — no new storage systems (§6.2). |

## Security posture

**Verify from the signed log alone.** Relay-side enforcement is an
optimization, never an assumption. Every consumer verifies events
(`Event.verify()`), validates attestations, and counts approvals itself via
the `verify` module. A deployment on Buzz gets enforcement as a bonus; a
deployment on a plain NIP-29 relay loses no *integrity*, only convenience.

**Capabilities are attested, not inferred.** Relay behaviors the protocol
does not guarantee — above all NIP-29 private-group *read* gating — must be
explicitly attested per deployment in config (`CAP_PRIVATE_READ_GATING`).
The relay client refuses to treat a channel as private until the deployment
attests enforcement: fail loud, never degrade silently.

**Privacy is a wire-format property.** Run accounting publishes one rolled-up
event per run — never per step; ephemeral workers author no events and
surface only in their persistent parent's subgraph digest. The API is shaped
so the private thing is the easy thing: there is no per-step publish surface
to misuse.

**Owner attestation is NIP-OA, reused as-is.** Lindenmayer mints no kind for
it — NIP-OA is an `auth` *tag* attachable to any event. The upstream schema
is restated in `docs/kinds/nip-oa-attestation.md` so draft churn cannot
retroactively change what historical attestations meant.

## Acceptable degradation (plain NIP-29 relay vs Buzz)

Per the relay-integration research (aggregate, recommendation 3), the
following degradations are accepted and documented rather than papered over:

- **Merge-gate enforcement is not this library's concern.** Approval
  request/verdict events (42040/42041) are signed records; *enforcement* of
  approval-gated merges is a bridge/process concern layered above them
  (Buzz's `buzz-protect` where present, process discipline elsewhere). The
  signed chain is complete either way — what varies is whether anything
  mechanically blocks a merge that ignores it.
- **Revocation has latency under bridge-refusal + reader-filtering.** Without
  relay-side identity enforcement, revoking a key means: the bridge refuses
  to publish for revoked keys (stops new activity at the source), and readers
  filter by attestation validity at query time (`verify` module — bounds
  trust in stored history). This reproduces Buzz's outcome with higher
  latency and procedural rather than structural guarantees. That latency
  window is real; consumers that care must apply read-time filtering — the
  library ships it, but cannot make a relay un-serve stale events.
- **Human surfaces are pure UX.** The packaged surfaces (desktop app,
  directory, huddles) are conveniences; any NIP-29 client can perform the
  same functions against the same events. Nothing recorded depends on them.

What does *not* degrade: everything Lindenmayer records — lifecycle history,
run accounting, digests, approval chains, template versions, attestations —
stores, verifies, and stays queryable on any NIP-01+29+42 relay.

## Key handling

Secrets resolve at runtime from an env var or a `chmod 600` key file
(`CoreConfig.load_keypair`); they never appear in config files, logs, or
reprs. Signing happens only in `keys` (libsecp256k1 via coincurve).

content.content:
[project]
name = "lindenmayer"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Control plane for governing Fractal agent subgraphs through Buzz"
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.12"
dependencies = [
    # Justified in the core node's plan (core_foundation):
    # coincurve — BIP-340 Schnorr over libsecp256k1 (the reference C implementation);
    #   the trust root must not ride a pure-Python curve implementation.
    "coincurve>=20.0",
    # pydantic — typed kind models with validation (accept/reject surface).
    "pydantic>=2.7",
    # websockets — mature asyncio WebSocket client for the relay client.
    "websockets>=13.0",
]

[dependency-groups]
dev = [
    "pytest>=8.0",
    "pytest-asyncio>=0.24",
]

[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"

[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
packages = ["src/lindenmayer"]
---
./pyproject.toml

content.content:
1	"""Event model tests: published vectors, round-trips, tamper detection.
2	
3	Vector provenance:
4	- tests/vectors/bip340_vectors.csv — the published BIP-340 test vectors from
5	  bitcoin/bips (bip-0340/test-vectors.csv), verbatim.
6	- tests/vectors/nip_oa_signed_event.json — the published signed NIP-01 event
7	  from block/buzz docs/nips/NIP-OA.md ("Signed Event Example"), verbatim.
8	  Its id and sig were produced by an independent implementation, which makes
9	  it an end-to-end NIP-01 id/sig vector for us.
10	"""
11	
12	import csv
13	import json
14	from pathlib import Path
15	
16	import pytest
17	
18	from lindenmayer.core import (
19	    Event,
20	    EventValidationError,
21	    Keypair,
22	    compute_event_id,
23	    schnorr_sign,
24	    schnorr_verify,
25	)
26	
27	VECTORS = Path(__file__).parent / "vectors"
28	
29	
30	def load_bip340_rows():
31	    with open(VECTORS / "bip340_vectors.csv", newline="") as fh:
32	        return list(csv.DictReader(fh))
33	
34	
35	def load_nip_oa_event() -> dict:
36	    return json.loads((VECTORS / "nip_oa_signed_event.json").read_text())
37	
38	
39	# -- BIP-340 published vectors -------------------------------------------
40	
41	
42	@pytest.mark.parametrize("row", load_bip340_rows(), ids=lambda r: f"vec{r['index']}")
43	def test_bip340_verify_vectors(row):
44	    """Every published vector's verification result must be reproduced."""
45	    expected = row["verification result"] == "TRUE"
46	    ok = schnorr_verify(
47	        row["public key"].lower(),
48	        bytes.fromhex(row["message"]),
49	        bytes.fromhex(row["signature"]),
50	    )
51	    assert ok is expected, row["comment"]
52	
53	
54	@pytest.mark.parametrize(
55	    "row",
56	    # Vectors 15-18 exercise BIP-340's variable-length message support, which
57	    # coincurve's signing API rejects (32-byte digests only). Nostr signs only
58	    # 32-byte SHA-256 event ids, so those vectors are out of scope for signing;
59	    # they still run through test_bip340_verify_vectors above.
60	    [r for r in load_bip340_rows() if r["secret key"] and len(r["message"]) == 64],
61	    ids=lambda r: f"vec{r['index']}",
62	)
63	def test_bip340_sign_vectors(row):
64	    """Signing with the vector's secret and aux randomness reproduces the
65	    vector signature exactly (BIP-340 signing is deterministic given aux)."""
66	    sig = schnorr_sign(
67	        bytes.fromhex(row["secret key"]),
68	        bytes.fromhex(row["message"]),
69	        aux_rand=bytes.fromhex(row["aux_rand"]),
70	    )
71	    assert sig.hex().upper() == row["signature"]
72	
73	
74	# -- NIP-01 id computation and full verification --------------------------
75	
76	
77	def test_nip01_id_matches_published_event():
78	    ev = load_nip_oa_event()
79	    assert (
80	        compute_event_id(ev["pubkey"], ev["created_at"], ev["kind"], ev["tags"], ev["content"])
81	        == ev["id"]
82	    )
83	
84	
85	def test_nip01_full_verify_published_event():
86	    event = Event.from_dict(load_nip_oa_event())
87	    assert event.id_valid()
88	    assert event.sig_valid()
89	    assert event.verify()
90	
91	
92	def test_nip01_canonical_escaping():
93	    """Content with quotes, backslashes, newlines, and non-ASCII must follow
94	    JSON.stringify-style minimal escaping (NIP-01)."""
95	    event_id = compute_event_id(
96	        "a" * 64, 1700000000, 1, [], 'quote " backslash \\ newline \n unicode é'
97	    )
98	    assert len(event_id) == 64
99	    # Independent recomputation with explicit expected serialization:
100	    import hashlib
101	
102	    expected = (
103	        '[0,"' + "a" * 64 + '",1700000000,1,[],'
104	        '"quote \\" backslash \\\\ newline \\n unicode é"]'
105	    ).encode()
106	    assert event_id == hashlib.sha256(expected).hexdigest()
107	
108	
109	# -- round-trips and tamper detection --------------------------------------
110	
111	
112	def test_sign_verify_roundtrip():
113	    kp = Keypair.generate()
114	    event = Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, tags=[["t", "test"]], content="hello")
115	    signed = kp.sign_event(event)
116	    assert signed.verify()
117	    # JSON round-trip preserves validity
118	    assert Event.from_json(signed.to_json()).verify()
119	
120	
121	def test_tampered_content_fails():
122	    kp = Keypair.generate()
123	    signed = kp.sign_event(Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, content="original"))
124	    data = signed.to_dict()
125	    data["content"] = "tampered"
126	    assert not Event.from_dict(data).verify()
127	
128	
129	def test_tampered_id_fails():
130	    kp = Keypair.generate()
131	    signed = kp.sign_event(Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, content="x"))
132	    data = signed.to_dict()
133	    data["id"] = "0" * 64
134	    assert not Event.from_dict(data).verify()
135	
136	
137	def test_wrong_key_signature_fails():
138	    kp, other = Keypair.generate(), Keypair.generate()
139	    event = Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, content="x")
140	    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
141	        other.sign_event(event)  # pubkey mismatch refused at signing time
142	
143	
144	def test_unsigned_event_does_not_verify():
145	    kp = Keypair.generate()
146	    assert not Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, content="x").verify()
147	
148	
149	# -- structural validation --------------------------------------------------
150	
151	
152	@pytest.mark.parametrize(
153	    "mutation",
154	    [
155	        {"pubkey": "not-hex"},
156	        {"pubkey": "AB" * 32},  # uppercase refused
157	        {"id": "00"},
158	        {"sig": "zz" * 64},
159	        {"created_at": "1700000000"},
160	        {"kind": 70000},

file.content:
"""Event model tests: published vectors, round-trips, tamper detection.

Vector provenance:
- tests/vectors/bip340_vectors.csv — the published BIP-340 test vectors from
  bitcoin/bips (bip-0340/test-vectors.csv), verbatim.
- tests/vectors/nip_oa_signed_event.json — the published signed NIP-01 event
  from block/buzz docs/nips/NIP-OA.md ("Signed Event Example"), verbatim.
  Its id and sig were produced by an independent implementation, which makes
  it an end-to-end NIP-01 id/sig vector for us.
"""

import csv
import json
from pathlib import Path

import pytest

from lindenmayer.core import (
    Event,
    EventValidationError,
    Keypair,
    compute_event_id,
    schnorr_sign,
    schnorr_verify,
)

VECTORS = Path(__file__).parent / "vectors"


def load_bip340_rows():
    with open(VECTORS / "bip340_vectors.csv", newline="") as fh:
        return list(csv.DictReader(fh))


def load_nip_oa_event() -> dict:
    return json.loads((VECTORS / "nip_oa_signed_event.json").read_text())


# -- BIP-340 published vectors -------------------------------------------


@pytest.mark.parametrize("row", load_bip340_rows(), ids=lambda r: f"vec{r['index']}")
def test_bip340_verify_vectors(row):
    """Every published vector's verification result must be reproduced."""
    expected = row["verification result"] == "TRUE"
    ok = schnorr_verify(
        row["public key"].lower(),
        bytes.fromhex(row["message"]),
        bytes.fromhex(row["signature"]),
    )
    assert ok is expected, row["comment"]


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "row",
    # Vectors 15-18 exercise BIP-340's variable-length message support, which
    # coincurve's signing API rejects (32-byte digests only). Nostr signs only
    # 32-byte SHA-256 event ids, so those vectors are out of scope for signing;
    # they still run through test_bip340_verify_vectors above.
    [r for r in load_bip340_rows() if r["secret key"] and len(r["message"]) == 64],
    ids=lambda r: f"vec{r['index']}",
)
def test_bip340_sign_vectors(row):
    """Signing with the vector's secret and aux randomness reproduces the
    vector signature exactly (BIP-340 signing is deterministic given aux)."""
    sig = schnorr_sign(
        bytes.fromhex(row["secret key"]),
        bytes.fromhex(row["message"]),
        aux_rand=bytes.fromhex(row["aux_rand"]),
    )
    assert sig.hex().upper() == row["signature"]


# -- NIP-01 id computation and full verification --------------------------


def test_nip01_id_matches_published_event():
    ev = load_nip_oa_event()
    assert (
        compute_event_id(ev["pubkey"], ev["created_at"], ev["kind"], ev["tags"], ev["content"])
        == ev["id"]
    )


def test_nip01_full_verify_published_event():
    event = Event.from_dict(load_nip_oa_event())
    assert event.id_valid()
    assert event.sig_valid()
    assert event.verify()


def test_nip01_canonical_escaping():
    """Content with quotes, backslashes, newlines, and non-ASCII must follow
    JSON.stringify-style minimal escaping (NIP-01)."""
    event_id = compute_event_id(
        "a" * 64, 1700000000, 1, [], 'quote " backslash \\ newline \n unicode é'
    )
    assert len(event_id) == 64
    # Independent recomputation with explicit expected serialization:
    import hashlib

    expected = (
        '[0,"' + "a" * 64 + '",1700000000,1,[],'
        '"quote \\" backslash \\\\ newline \\n unicode é"]'
    ).encode()
    assert event_id == hashlib.sha256(expected).hexdigest()


# -- round-trips and tamper detection --------------------------------------


def test_sign_verify_roundtrip():
    kp = Keypair.generate()
    event = Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, tags=[["t", "test"]], content="hello")
    signed = kp.sign_event(event)
    assert signed.verify()
    # JSON round-trip preserves validity
    assert Event.from_json(signed.to_json()).verify()


def test_tampered_content_fails():
    kp = Keypair.generate()
    signed = kp.sign_event(Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, content="original"))
    data = signed.to_dict()
    data["content"] = "tampered"
    assert not Event.from_dict(data).verify()


def test_tampered_id_fails():
    kp = Keypair.generate()
    signed = kp.sign_event(Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, content="x"))
    data = signed.to_dict()
    data["id"] = "0" * 64
    assert not Event.from_dict(data).verify()


def test_wrong_key_signature_fails():
    kp, other = Keypair.generate(), Keypair.generate()
    event = Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, content="x")
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        other.sign_event(event)  # pubkey mismatch refused at signing time


def test_unsigned_event_does_not_verify():
    kp = Keypair.generate()
    assert not Event.build(pubkey=kp.public_key_hex, kind=1, content="x").verify()


# -- structural validation --------------------------------------------------


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "mutation",
    [
        {"pubkey": "not-hex"},
        {"pubkey": "AB" * 32},  # uppercase refused
        {"id": "00"},
        {"sig": "zz" * 64},
        {"created_at": "1700000000"},
        {"kind": 70000},
        {"kind": True},
        {"content": 5},
        {"tags": [["ok"], "not-a-list"]},
        {"tags": [[1, 2]]},
    ],
)
def test_from_dict_rejects_malformed(mutation):
    data = load_nip_oa_event() | mutation
    with pytest.raises(EventValidationError):
        Event.from_dict(data)


def test_tag_access():
    event = Event.build(
        pubkey="a" * 64,
        kind=42010,
        tags=[["branch", "main.core"], ["status", "active"], ["e", "f" * 64, "relay"]],
        content="",
    )
    assert event.first_tag_value("branch") == "main.core"
    assert event.first_tag_value("missing") is None
    assert list(event.tag_values("e")) == [("f" * 64, "relay")]

set -euo pipefail

# Run the node's test suite; exit 0 on success, non-zero on failure
# -----------------------------------------------------------------

# No-op by default; extend per node with the project's test command.

# Run the library test suite from the worktree root.
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd -P)"
WORKTREE_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../../.." && pwd)"
cd "$WORKTREE_DIR"
UV_PYTHON=3.13 uv run --inexact pytest tests/ -q
---SETUP---
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Environment setup -- runs at the start of every iteration
# ---------------------------------------------------------
#
# Must be idempotent. Update this script instead of installing
# packages inline, so the environment stays reproducible across
# iterations. The loop activates the repo venv automatically.

# Sync the project venv into the worktree-local .venv (Python 3.13 —
# coincurve ships no cp314 wheels; the shared repo .venv stays untouched).
if command -v uv &>/dev/null; then
    UV_PYTHON=3.13 uv sync --inexact --quiet
fi

file.content:
---
name: node_operations
desc: Repo-specific fractal operating quirks every node hits (scope, transcripts, spawning).
created: 2026-07-23T13:28:12Z
updated: 2026-07-23T13:28:12Z
---

# node_operations

Operating facts specific to this repo that differ from stock fractal
behavior. Any node committing or spawning here will hit these.

## Transcripts vs scope

A hook syncs the live session transcript to `transcripts/<session>.jsonl`
(untracked). For any node with a scope set, this file is out of scope and
`fractal commit` rejects it. Commit it deliberately with
`fractal commit --ignore-scope "<msg>"` (still lints) rather than leaving it
to the loop's `(auto)` force-commit backstop — transcripts are an
intentional public record for this tree (see `docs/DESIGN.md` §7).

## `.fractal/` ignore rules (changed on main)

The repo's `.gitignore` no longer blanket-ignores `.fractal/` — the entry
was dropped on main (commit 9e32acb) because it broke `fractal commit` for
every scoped node: fractal's staging names the node's own
`.fractal/<branch>` dir as an explicit literal pathspec, and git hard-errors
on an explicitly-named ignored path. Runtime-state exclusion is fractal's
own `.git/info/exclude` mechanism, which is narrower. Consequences:

- Scoped `fractal commit` works without `--force` again; stop routing
  around it.
- `.fractal/` may appear as untracked in `git status` — that is expected,
  not drift. Nothing under `.fractal/` is tracked on any branch.
- Versioned node contracts still live in `tree/` (see CLAUDE.md); a child's
  NODE.md/memory/plans are edited on disk in the child's worktree, and
  merge-up carries only project files.

## rtk filters merge commits from `git log`

The rtk proxy (which transparently wraps git commands) drops merge commits
from `git log` output — after a PREPARE merge, HEAD can look stale or your
merge commits invisible. Not data loss: verify with
`rtk proxy git log --first-parent --oneline` (raw, unfiltered) or
`git rev-parse HEAD` before concluding a merge didn't land.

## CLI syntax gotchas

- `fractal commit` takes the message positionally (no `-m`).
- `fractal radio reply` takes no `--subject` (inherits the parent
  message's).

input.content:
# 11.1 Relay client implementation

Leaf node (max_children=0) — solo execution, no spawning.

## Scope

Build `src/lindenmayer/core/relay.py` (asyncio client on `websockets`,
NIP-01 + NIP-29 + NIP-42 minimum contract) and `tests/test_relay.py` plus a
mock-relay test helper. Nothing else under `src/` or `tests/` is touched.

## Design

**`RelayClient`** (asyncio, one connection per instance):

- `__init__(relay_url, keypair, config)` — stores `CoreConfig` for capability
  checks; no connection yet.
- `connect()` / `close()` / `async with` context manager — opens the
  `websockets` connection, starts a background reader task that dispatches
  incoming frames by type (`EVENT`, `EOSE`, `OK`, `NOTICE`, `CLOSED`, `AUTH`)
  to per-subscription queues / OK-waiters / an auth-challenge future.
- `publish(event, group=None)` — adds `h` tag if `group` given (raise if the
  event already carries an `h` tag conflicting), sends `["EVENT", event_dict]`,
  awaits the matching `OK` via a future keyed by event id, raises
  `RelayRejection` on `ok=False` (fail loud — never swallow). On an `auth-required:`
  NOTICE/OK prefix (NIP-42 convention) with no prior successful auth, wait for
  the `AUTH` challenge, respond, then retry the publish once.
- `query(filters, group=None)` — adds `h` tag to each filter dict if `group`,
  sends `["REQ", sub_id, *filters]`, collects verified `EVENT`s into a list
  until `EOSE`, sends `["CLOSE", sub_id]`, returns the list. Invalid-signature
  events are dropped and counted (`self.dropped_invalid`), never yielded.
- `subscribe(filters, group=None)` — same REQ setup but returns an async
  generator that yields verified events indefinitely (post-EOSE too) until
  `unsubscribe`/generator close, which sends CLOSE.
- Auth: `_handle_auth_challenge(challenge)` builds kind-22242 event
  (`relay`, `challenge` tags), signs via keypair, publishes it (bare EVENT +
  OK wait, no group), resolves the pending auth future/state.
- **Private gating**: `require_private(group)` (or a `private=True` kwarg on
  publish/subscribe/query) raises `PrivateCapabilityError` unless
  `config.has_capability(CAP_PRIVATE_READ_GATING)`. Non-private calls
  (the default) are unaffected — this is an explicit opt-in surface, not a
  wrapper around every call.
- Reconnect: bounded retry loop with exponential backoff on connection drop;
  on reconnect, re-issue `REQ` for all live subscriptions (no replay of
  missed events — no persistent queue, documented in the module docstring
  and `README.md`... actually README.md is core's, out of my ownership, so
  document only in relay.py's own docstring).

Exceptions: `RelayError` (base), `RelayRejection` (OK false), `PrivateCapabilityError`.

## Mock relay (tests/relay_mock.py, or similar — my own file, no naming clash)

An in-process `websockets.serve` server:
- EVENT -> validates minimally, stores in a dict, replies OK true (config:
  optionally OK false to test rejection).
- REQ -> filters stored events (kind/authors/kinds/#h tag match), sends
  EVENT... then EOSE; keeps subscription alive for live fan-out on later
  EVENT publishes matching the filter.
- CLOSE -> drops subscription.
- Configurable auth-required mode: rejects ops with `["OK", id, false,
  "auth-required: ..."]` / `["CLOSED", sub_id, "auth-required: ..."]` until
  the connection sends a valid signed kind-22242 AUTH event matching a
  challenge the mock sent via `["AUTH", challenge]` on connect.

## Test coverage (tests/test_relay.py)

1. publish -> OK round-trip (success and explicit rejection raises).
2. query collects stored events until EOSE.
3. subscribe yields live-published events.
4. NIP-42 auth flow: mock demands auth, client authenticates, retried op
   succeeds.
5. Invalid-signature inbound event is dropped, not delivered (assert dropped
   counter increments / event never surfaces in query or subscribe results).
6. `h`-tag scoping: group param adds `h` tag on publish and on filters; mock
   asserts/filters on it.
7. Private-gating: `require_private`/`private=True` raises
   `PrivateCapabilityError` without attestation; succeeds (or at least
   doesn't raise) with a `CAP_PRIVATE_READ_GATING` attestation in `CoreConfig`.

## Budget check

~$0.85 spent of $2/iter cap so far orienting; plenty of iterations (8 total)
and $4.9 remaining at node level. Implementation is a single focused file —
solo, no spawn triggers apply (task is not separable, no parallelizable
independent subtasks, no need for a clean-context child). Proceed directly
to EXECUTE this iteration; expect this to take 1-2 iterations to land tests
green.
